name: Domestic-Clinical Balance in Physical Environment
consequences: The residential setting preserves naturalistic parasomnia expression — clinical environments suppress these events — but creates trade-offs in staffing safety margins, noise/alarm false-positive rates, and regulatory justification for emergency protocols. Domestic design choices (aesthetic finishes, residential furniture, subdued lighting) directly influence the quality and frequency of captured events, yet increase complexity of fire safety certification and staff fatigue management during overnight incident response.
options: Maintain the quiet Bonn residential property with minimal clinical signage, standard household fixtures, and soft ambient lighting, accepting higher baseline false-alarm rates from domestic noise and requiring stricter technician training for distinguishing behavioral events from environmental artifacts.

Retrofit the facility with discrete clinical safety infrastructure (reinforced door frames, pressure-sensitive floor mats, silent infrared motion barriers) while preserving visual residential aesthetics, adding €40–60K capital cost and requiring a dual-certification pathway (residential building and clinical research facility) that extends ethics approval by 6–8 weeks.

Operate a 4-suite clinical research wing adjacent to the main residential area, housing participants flagged post-admission as high-injury risk or with recurrent polysomnography-confirmed seizure-like events, enabling role-specific design while maintaining residential space for the majority of lower-risk NREM parasomnia cohort.
review: The core tension is that clinical safety measures (electronic monitoring, bright lighting, standardized alarms) objectively increase capture reliability but likely reduce spontaneous parasomnia frequency — the variable you most need to measure. The options above assume staffing capability and regulatory tolerance to manage this trade-off, but they all sidestep the hardest question: at what threshold of event suppression does a clinically safer environment produce data so artifact-laden or event-poor that publications lack credibility? None of the options directly measure or pre-specify acceptable baseline event-loss thresholds.

name: Tiered Sensing Acquisition: Continuity vs. Resolution Trade-Off
consequences: The current plan commits to continuous lightweight monitoring (dry EEG, mattress sensors, infrared, microphone, environmental data) plus selective full PSG on three fixed nights per participant and triggered escalation nights. This tiered approach balances participant burden and cost against the physiological resolution needed for event classification, but creates a bottleneck: technician flagging accuracy determines escalation yield, and if false-negative event detection during lightweight nights exceeds 15–20%, the full PSG nights miss recurrent events and Aim 2 phenotyping becomes underpowered.
options: Implement continuous full PSG montage (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, bilateral leg EMG) across all 8 active suites for all nights, frontloading approximately €180K equipment cost and adding €35–45K annual technician annotation labor, ensuring no event-miss bias but concentrating all resource risk in the first year and requiring 9–12 month vendor lead times for 8 PSG systems.

Commit to lightweight continuous sensing on all nights plus a fixed fourth full PSG night (night 5 of the stay) selected statistically to capture mid-sequence clustering patterns, maintaining current capital cost while adding one scheduled full-montage night per participant and allowing technicians to flag escalation nights asynchronously rather than in real-time, reducing on-shift cognitive load.

Operate a two-tier participant pathway: Tier A (n=25, NREM parasomnia confirmed on screening PSG with high baseline frequency) receives 3 full-PSG nights; Tier B (n=30–40, infrequent or newly suspected parasomnia) receives continuous lightweight monitoring only plus one full-PSG night, reducing equipment and labor costs by 30–40% while preserving statistical power for frequent-event phenotyping through the larger Tier B cohort.
review: This lever's core tension is the signal-to-noise ratio: continuous lightweight sensing gives broad temporal coverage but cannot definitively classify events without full EMG and EEG; selective full PSG is definitive but samples only 3–5 of 14–56 available nights per participant, risking a miss rate that invalidates longitudinal conclusions. The options above accept different miss tolerances, but none explicitly specify an a priori acceptable false-negative rate or validate that the chosen technician flagging algorithm (currently unspecified) will achieve it. Algorithm development is deferred to Aim 3, yet the decision to trigger escalation nights depends critically on that same algorithm's early-stage sensitivity.

name: Event Annotation and Scoring Bottleneck Management
consequences: Independent dual scoring of all captured events against AASM criteria is non-negotiable for publication credibility, but each full-PSG night generates 6–8 hours of multi-channel data requiring approximately 45–90 minutes of expert time per night for initial review and formal scoring. With 50–70 participants, 3–4 full PSG nights each, plus escalation nights, the annotation load reaches 200–250 full PSG nights across 3 years; two independent raters are €180–220K labor cost over the project, and misalignment or extended vacations create cascading delays in mid-study publication timelines and Aim 3 algorithm training data availability.
options: Hire two full-time research technicians with RPSGT certification in the first year for annotation, training them in AASM scoring and embedding them within the facility to enable real-time feedback loops with overnight technicians and reduce scoring lag to <2 weeks post-capture; budget €280K over 3 years and accept that both are office-based during daytime, reducing overnight shift rotation coverage unless a third technician is added.

Establish a contractual arrangement with the University of Bonn's sleep center or a neighboring hospital's accredited sleep laboratory to subcontract dual-scoring services for all PSG data, maintaining internal real-time annotation for lightweight-sensor flagging only; cost €220–260K but transfers expertise risk and creates dependency on external partner availability, which could delay publication by 4–12 weeks if the partner faces staffing turnover.

Develop a hybrid model: internal technicians score all full-PSG nights for real-time event flagging and preliminary review within 48 hours; a second independent scorer (recruited on flexible hourly contract from nearby university neurology or sleep research programs) reviews 30% of all nights (randomly stratified by participant and time period) for formal inter-rater reliability reporting, with discrepancies adjudicated by the PI; reduces permanent headcount to 1 FTE scorer at €100K but requires robust audit and conflict-resolution protocols.
review: The core tension is staffing density: more internal raters improve throughput and reduce external dependency, but a 9-person permanent team on a €3.8M budget is already front-loaded with salary costs (~€2M), and adding another FTE pushes personnel to 55% of budget. The options trade certainty of annotation speed against financial sustainability and external partnership risk. None of the options address the scenario where a single senior rater leaves mid-project — continuity risk is acknowledged but not mitigated by any of these choices, so a fourth option (backup rater training or cross-facility shadowing) should be considered outside this lever.

name: Recruitment Pathway Selectivity and Exclusion Threshold Calibration
consequences: The current plan targets NREM parasomnia confirmed through clinical interview and collateral history, with explicit exclusions for suspected epilepsy, untreated sleep apnea, active substance use disorder, psychiatric instability, and injury risk beyond facility capability. This approach aims to enroll a clinically representative cohort but risks excluding higher-yield participants (e.g., those with frequent but violent episodes) and creates ambiguity in the pre-admission adjudication process — the PI must adjudicate each exclusion case, introducing potential bias and slowing screening throughput. The trade-off: stricter inclusion criteria reduce heterogeneity but lower enrollment velocity and event yield; looser criteria accelerate recruitment but increase on-site incident management burden and potential ethics violations.
options: Maintain strict pre-admission screening with protocol-specified exclusion thresholds (e.g., PSA of >15 on screening, self-reported injury to self or others in past 6 months, active psychiatric medication changes within 3 months) adjudicated by PI and sleep medicine co-investigator together, requiring consensus for any borderline case; slows screening by 1–2 weeks per participant but reduces on-site safety variance and ensures Aim 2 cohort is clinically homogeneous for phenotyping.

Adopt a tiered recruitment model: Tier 1 (standard inclusion, n=35–40) uses the strict criteria above; Tier 2 (expanded, n=15–25) admits participants with mild sleep apnea (PSA 15–30) or stable psychiatric history provided psychiatry co-sign is obtained and extended safety briefing is documented, increasing enrollment speed by 25–35% and capturing broader phenotypic variation while requiring enhanced overnight incident protocols.

Delegate pre-admission screening to a specialist sleep medicine clinician hired as part of the recruitment team (distinct from the PI), standardizing the exclusion adjudication checklist and reducing PI bottleneck by 3–4 hours per week; enables parallel screening of multiple candidates and accommodates recruitment surges, but introduces potential variability if the clinician leaves and requires written decision rationale audit to ensure inter-rater stability with successor.
review: The core tension is recruitment velocity versus incident risk: the 50–70 participant enrollment target over 3 years implies 17–23 admissions per year on average, yet the current screening is not parallelized (PI adjudicates serially) and typical academic recruitment conversion rates are 40–60%, requiring screening of 85–175 referrals annually. The options above make different bets on risk tolerance and staffing investment. None explicitly addresses the downstream question: if Tier 2 participants produce a 3–5% higher on-site incident rate (e.g., nocturnal confusion leading to unsafe behavior), does the additional phenotypic diversity justify publication sensitivity or create liability exposure that requires post-hoc exclusion from analyses, nullifying the recruitment gain?

name: Matched Control Group Scope and Benchmarking Strategy
consequences: A matched control cohort provides essential baseline data on false-positive sensor triggers (e.g., mattress sensor noise during normal sleep, microphone detection of sleep-stage-related vocalizations) and ambient false alarms, directly enabling benchmarking of the Aim 3 event-triage algorithm. The current plan implies controls are necessary but does not specify their size or recruitment pathway; if controls are n=20–25 (matching the NREM parasomnia cohort demographics), the additional 200–250 participant-nights add approximately €16–20K compensation cost and 80–120 hours of dual-scoring time to quantify baseline sensor specificity. This controls cost is not explicitly budgeted and may compete with parasomnia participant enrollment if recruitment is slow.
options: Enroll a formal matched control group of n=20–25 healthy adults without sleep disorders, recruited from Bonn community postings and University of Bonn staff, undergoing the identical residential stay (2–4 weeks) with identical lightweight and selective full-PSG sensing; cost approximately €18–22K in compensation and scoring labor but provides rigorous baseline specificity estimates and publishable comparison statistics (e.g., 'false-positive rate of continuous mattress sensing in controls was 3.2 events per night vs. 0.8 in NREM parasomnia participants').

Retrospectively leverage existing polysomnography data from the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic: identify 15–20 participants with normal PSG studies and unambiguous sleep architecture, extract de-identified segments for mock lightweight-sensor simulation and algorithm validation, avoiding additional recruitment and compensation costs but sacrificing the true residential environment context and requiring archival data quality assessment that may introduce missing channels or time-synchronization artifacts.

Implement a rolling mini-control approach: enroll n=8–10 control participants in cohort batches (every 6–9 months), using their overnight sensor data to continuously validate algorithm performance thresholds and sensitivity-specificity operating points rather than treating controls as a fixed baseline group; spreads control recruitment across the project timeline, reducing initial-phase bottleneck, but complicates comparative statistical reporting and requires iterative algorithm re-validation as control data accumulates.
review: The core tension is statistical rigor versus resource competition: a formal matched control group provides unambiguous benchmarking for the event-triage algorithm and publishable specificity estimates, but the 20–25 control participants consume approximately 5–7% of the total recruitment and scoring budget with no direct scientific yield to the NREM parasomnia phenotyping (Aim 2). If enrollment lags and the PI faces a choice between admitting another NREM parasomnia participant or a control, controls will be deprioritized, leaving algorithm validation weaker at publication time. None of the options above propose an early-stage surrogate (e.g., synthetic sensor noise injection for initial algorithm training), which could reduce control cohort size without sacrificing algorithm calibration.

name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Algorithm Development: Scope and Validation Strategy
consequences: Aim 3 commits to developing a semi-automated tool that ranks probable parasomnia episodes and reduces manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than autonomous diagnosis. This scope is deliberately conservative — the tool is a screening/prioritization aid, not a diagnostic classifier — but its utility depends critically on achieving sufficient sensitivity (capturing at least 90% of true events, ideally >95%) to reduce technician false-negative risk during real-time flagging. Algorithm development requires labeled training data from Aim 2 (captured and adjudicated events) and must iterate through early participants; if the first 15–20 participants yield fewer than 50–60 independently adjudicated events, the training set is too small and algorithm performance may not reach operational utility by month 24, forcing a scope reduction (e.g., publish sensitivity-specificity estimates only, defer clinical implementation guidance to phase two).
options: Allocate 40% of the postdoctoral computational neuroscience researcher's time starting in month 6 (after the first cohort's data is annotated) to develop a machine-learning event-detection pipeline using multi-channel lightweight-sensor inputs, conducting 5-fold cross-validation on accumulated adjudicated events and reporting sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual scorers at 18 and 36 months; defer external algorithm validation to phase two and focus on transparency reporting (confusion matrices, per-sensor contribution) rather than clinical deployment.

Commission a specialized biomedical data science consultant (estimated €60–80K over 2 years) to lead algorithm development in parallel with internal Aim 2 work, using established open-source frameworks (e.g., Brainstorm, MNE-Python) and publishing intermediate results as methods papers; accelerates development timeline and leverages external expertise but introduces dependency on external contractor availability and requires explicit data-sharing agreements and intellectual property clarity with the university.

Establish a collaborative partnership with a sleep research informatics lab at a neighboring institution (e.g., Max Planck Institute, University of Cologne) to jointly develop the event-triage algorithm using the Bonn facility's labeled parasomnia data, with the partner lab providing computational expertise and statistical rigor in exchange for co-authorship and access to anonymized data; distributes workload and strengthens publication impact but complicates data governance, extends decision-making timelines due to coordination overhead, and creates multi-institutional ethics and legal compliance requirements.
review: The core tension is algorithm maturity versus publication urgency: Aim 3 explicitly disclaims autonomous diagnosis and commits to benchmarking against dual human scoring, which is methodologically sound but limits the algorithm's immediate clinical applicability and publishable novelty. If the first 20 participants yield only 30–40 adjudicated events due to low event-capture frequency, the training set is underpowered and cross-validation estimates will have wide confidence intervals, making the tool's operating characteristics unsuitable for even semi-automated clinical use. The options above accept different timelines and external dependency risks, but none include a contingency plan for insufficient training data (e.g., if event yield is below protocol threshold by month 18, Aim 3 scope is narrowed to descriptive reporting). Algorithm utility also depends on real-time technician buy-in — a complex tool that generates false alarms will be ignored, but this usability factor is not explicitly tested in any of the options.

name: Facility Expansion Timeline and Phase-Two Readiness Conditions
consequences: The current plan defers expansion from 8 to 12 suites until the pilot demonstrates acceptable data quality, false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and sustainable annotation throughput; this staged approach reduces capital risk and allows course correction before scaling. However, the decision to expand is inherently fuzzy — no quantitative metrics are specified for 'acceptable' false alarm rates or 'manageable' annotation throughput — and delaying expansion risks forfeiting recruitment momentum and missing the window for phase-two grant submissions (typically submitted 12–18 months before phase-one completion to enable seamless continuation). If the facility reaches operational stability by month 18–20 but expansion approval is delayed due to governance review cycles, the full 8-suite capacity may be underutilized in year 3, inflating per-participant overhead.
options: Specify quantitative go/no-go expansion thresholds at month 12 (e.g., data quality pass rate >92%, environmental false alarm rate <0.5 per suite-night, dual-scoring completion within 3 weeks of capture, zero serious adverse events) adjudicated by the external advisory board; if thresholds are met, execute suite 9–12 build-out and staffing expansion in parallel with month 13–18 operations, enabling full 12-suite operation by month 20 and allowing participant enrollment to accelerate in year 3.

Maintain flexibility by not committing to specific go/no-go metrics but establishing a standing decision gate at month 18 where the PI presents pilot outcomes (data quality, annotation velocity, incident rates, participant feedback, Aim 2 preliminary findings) to the advisory board and decides expansion case-by-case; preserves agility but creates ambiguity for phase-two grant planning and may lead to last-minute staffing and renovation logistics if expansion is greenlit late.

Parallel-path expansion planning: contract with the facility's landlord in year 1 to secure an option to lease suites 9–12 with a 6-month notice clause (contingent on phase-two funding), and begin phase-two grant proposal development in month 18 using preliminary Aim 2 results as evidence of scientific feasibility; decouples operational expansion from funding approval and allows the grant proposal to request expansion capital as part of phase two, aligning staffing and renovation with external funding certainty.
review: The core tension is between premature scaling (over-building before operational model is proven) and momentum loss (delayed expansion that underutilizes capacity and misses publication and recruitment windows). The current plan is intentionally conservative, which reduces financial risk, but the vagueness of 'acceptable' metrics and lack of explicit timeline for phase-two grant submission could result in operational bottlenecks in year 3 when event-capture demand exceeds 8-suite throughput. None of the options above address the staffing constraint: scaling to 12 suites without proportional technician expansion (from 3 to 4.5 FTE overnight shift coverage) would increase false-negative event detection risk and violate safe staffing protocols, yet the additional €80–100K salary cost may not be approved mid-project if initial funding is strictly allocated.

name: Recruitment Speed-to-Certainty Trade-Off
consequences: Relaxing pre-admission diagnostic stringency (e.g., accepting self-reported parasomnia without mandatory prior polysomnography) accelerates enrollment and reduces per-participant screening overhead, but increases the risk of admitting participants with mimics (e.g., nocturnal seizures, RBD misclassified as NREM parasomnia) who produce ambiguous or unusable data and consume facility days. A higher false-inclusion rate directly shortens the window for capturing true parasomnia events and delays algorithm training.
options: Require specialist clinical interview plus collateral history plus prior polysomnography or screening PSG at admission for all NREM parasomnia recruits, accepting slower but high-confidence enrollment

Accept self-reported parasomnia history with a single consultative EEG screen during the first admission night, with a protocol-defined quarantine period to reassess participants whose initial recordings show epileptiform activity or other mimics

Implement a two-tier recruitment model: fast-track admission for participants with recent (within 12 months) clinical polysomnography documenting parasomnia events, and standard-track for others, with separate cohort analysis to assess data-quality differences
review: The tension is enrollment velocity versus data quality and participant safety. All three options assume recruitment channels and PI screening capacity remain constant, but none addresses the possibility that regional neurologist referral networks may have pre-existing biases (e.g., epilepsy specialists may disproportionately refer suspected mimics), so relaxed diagnostic thresholds at admission could be offset by upstream referral patterns that the project does not control. A fourth pathway — partnering with a German sleep medicine network to establish a shared pre-screening hub — is not included but could decouple referral quality from individual-facility diagnostic rigor.

name: Night-Shift Staffing Redundancy and Retention Model
consequences: The facility requires 3 technicians rotating night shifts to cover 8 active suites safely; a single unexpected absence leaves the facility understaffed for event response and annotation, forcing cancellations or deferred data scoring. Overnight shift work in Germany is subject to strict statutory rest requirements and social insurance penalties, and technician burnout is predictable in a job requiring 24/7 real-time monitoring, rapid decision-making, and dual-scoring annotation. Staffing instability directly undermines enrollment continuity and data quality.
options: Hire 3 full-time permanent night technicians with competitive salary adjustments (12–15% premium) and guaranteed shift rotation schedules with statutory rest compliance, accepting fixed headcount and cost escalation but minimizing turnover risk

Contract with a specialized medical staffing agency to supply on-call night technicians rotating through the facility, reducing fixed payroll but introducing dependency on external scheduling, potential quality variance, and loss of institutional knowledge about participant-specific annotation practices

Create a hybrid model: maintain 2 core permanent technicians plus 1 flexible pool technician (part-time or on-call) with cross-training incentives and performance bonuses for shift coverage, reducing fixed costs while preserving continuity, though introducing scheduling complexity and potential two-person night shifts on high-turnover weeks
review: The core tension is predictable cost and retention versus operational flexibility and lean budgeting. None of the three options directly addresses burnout mitigation beyond pay: the job's cognitive load (real-time event spotting, dual-scoring review, safety monitoring) and circadian stress are intrinsic. A substantive path missing from all options is task-shifting — deploying an evening shift of data analysts to pre-stage preliminary event flagging before the night technician's shift, which would reduce real-time annotation burden but requires additional staffing and introduces a new handoff dependency.

name: De-Identified Data Sharing Scope and Repository Strategy
consequences: Depositing de-identified raw physiological and sensor data in a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, PhysioNet) amplifies scientific impact, enables competitive secondary analysis, and attracts pharmaceutical partnership interest; however, bedroom video remains restricted, and time-series patterns from frequent nocturnal arousal or movement events could theoretically be re-identified when combined with external metadata (e.g., published participant demographics, family pedigrees). Choosing a restrictive data-use agreement (DUA) model preserves privacy but limits reuse and delays downstream research.
options: Release fully de-identified polysomnography and sensor time series under a standard public CC-BY license to OpenNeuro or equivalent, with no DUA requirement, accepting residual re-identification risk in exchange for maximum scientific access and visibility

Deposit data under a controlled-access model requiring institutional DUA signature, with de-identified data accessible only to credentialed researchers whose intended use is reviewed by the project's data committee, reducing re-identification risk and providing oversight but introducing 4–8 week review delays per new request

Publish aggregated summary statistics and event-count distributions in peer-reviewed papers, plus offer raw participant-level data only upon direct written request to the PI with explanation of intended analysis, retaining full curation control but limiting reuse and discouraging exploratory secondary research
review: The tension is scientific impact and data-driven precision versus privacy risk and institutional liability. None of the three options addresses the specific challenge that parasomnia phenotypes are themselves rare and may carry stigma, so even anonymized time-series patterns linked to a publication could enable family members or social networks to identify a known individual with sleepwalking. A missing pathway is time-locked release — restricting public access for 2–3 years post-publication, giving the primary team priority for high-impact work while eventually opening data, but this defers community benefit and is not captured.

name: Matched Control Group Scale and Phenotypic Overlap
consequences: A larger matched-control cohort (n=20–30) provides dense baseline data for sleep-stage transitions, arousal architecture, and sensor false-positives under normal conditions, enabling more robust algorithm benchmarking and confidence intervals; however, recruiting non-symptomatic controls requires separate ethics approval, additional compensation, extended facility occupancy, and careful age/sex matching, adding €150–250K and 8–12 weeks. A minimal control group (n=8–10) reduces cost and schedule but provides sparse comparison data and limits the algorithmic sensitivity and specificity estimates that journals expect.
options: Enroll a matched control group of 8–10 participants, stratified by age decade and sex to the NREM parasomnia cohort, completing residential monitoring within months 12–18, prioritizing cost efficiency and schedule adherence over statistical power for benchmarking

Enroll a larger matched control group of 20–30 participants over the full 3-year period with detailed phenotypic matching on sleep history, caffeine/alcohol exposure, and occupational factors, deploying a rigorous bootstrap comparison framework for algorithm tuning and accepting delayed publication of algorithm validation results

Secure a separate collaborative data-sharing agreement with an existing sleep research center in Germany or Europe to leverage their already-collected normative polysomnography cohort (n=50+) as controls, eliminating in-house control recruitment but introducing dependency on external data provenance, annotation standards, and publication timelines
review: The tension is algorithmic rigor and statistical power versus budget containment and schedule closure. All three options assume controls will be healthy sleepers with no prior parasomnia history, but the project does not specify whether controls will undergo the same enhanced PSG protocol (first/mid/final nights) or only baseline-night monitoring — if controls receive lighter sensing, the comparison is not apples-to-apples for false-positive rate estimation. A missing option is using the initial 5–8 NREM parasomnia participants' baseline (non-event) nights as algorithmic training controls, which would defer control recruitment but risks overfitting the algorithm to participants whose pathologic arousal architecture is already present.

name: Publication Timing Strategy and Competitive Positioning
consequences: Publishing preliminary methods-validation results (Aim 1: facility safety, operational validation) at month 18–20 establishes priority and builds reputation, attracting clinical referrals and potential pharma interest, but premature disclosure may alert competitors or give pharmaceutical sponsors incomplete data before they commit resources. Withholding publication until complete Aim 2 (longitudinal phenotyping) and Aim 3 (algorithm benchmarking) results are ready ensures more comprehensive and impactful papers, but delays visibility and risks competitors publishing similar parasomnia methodologies or algorithms first, potentially scooping key findings.
options: Publish a methods-validation manuscript in a secondary sleep medicine journal by month 18 covering safety, technical performance, and initial enrollment metrics, while reserving core parasomnia phenotyping and algorithm results for high-impact journals at month 28–30, balancing early visibility with downstream impact

Implement a coordinated manuscript roadmap with 5–6 papers timed to months 24, 28, 32, and 36, covering methods (month 24), longitudinal phenotyping phase-one (month 28), algorithm benchmarking (month 32), and cohort synthesis (month 36), maximizing publication frequency and sustained visibility across the grant period

Adopt a sequential-confidentiality agreement with pharmaceutical partners whereby algorithm and phenotyping manuscripts remain under 30-day pre-publication review until month 26, after which embargo restrictions lift regardless of partner feedback, protecting negotiating room while guaranteeing academic publication autonomy and mitigating the risk of indefinite delay
review: The tension is first-mover advantage and reputation building versus manuscript impact and scooping risk. None of the three options addresses the scenario that Aim 2 (phenotyping) and Aim 3 (algorithm) may produce iterative refinements during months 20–28, invalidating early methodology papers if the final sensing or annotation protocol diverges significantly from the pilot. A pathway absent from all options is preregistration on a public clinical-trials database (ClinicalTrials.gov or similar) with a mandatory publication commitment by month 36, which would reduce selective reporting bias but is not typical for methods-development research and may deter pharmaceutical interest.

name: Event-Capture Sufficiency Threshold and Stay Duration Ceiling
consequences: Extending maximum participant stay from 10 weeks to 14 weeks increases the likelihood of capturing at least one parasomnia event (from current 70–80% per the plan to potentially 85–90%) and provides richer within-person longitudinal data, but consumes proportionally more facility days, participant compensation (~€560 additional per extended participant), and technician time. If the true capture rate is already 75%, extending stays adds cost without proportional event yield, whereas if the true rate is lower (e.g., 55%), the extension becomes critical. Conversely, tightening the threshold and capping at 8 weeks accelerates participant turnover and enrollment velocity but risks high unproductive-admission rates that trigger recruitment-criteria review and operational instability.
options: Maintain the planned 2–8 week initial stay plus up to 2 additional weeks on-demand (10-week ceiling total), with a protocol review triggered if unproductive admissions exceed 20%, and adjust recruitment criteria iteratively based on month-6 and month-12 capture-rate estimates

Implement a time-to-event analysis framework: admit participants for a minimum 10-week stay by default, with week 11–14 extension available only if the participant has captured 0 events by day 63 and the technician and PI jointly assess likelihood of capture in the extended window, using historical event-frequency data from the same participant's screening interview to inform the decision

Impose a strict 8-week maximum stay per participant with no extension option, with capture-rate data reviewed at months 6 and 12; if unproductive admissions exceed 15%, immediately tighten inclusion criteria (e.g., require 4+ self-reported episodes per month instead of 2+), accepting higher rejection rates but maximizing facility throughput and data efficiency
review: The tension is event-yield security versus throughput and cost containment. All three options assume that extended stays do not systematically suppress event frequency through habituation or reduced sleep quality (i.e., that a 14-week stay captures events at the same rate as an 8-week stay), but the plan provides no evidence on whether institutional effects or sleep-environment adaptation influence event expression over time. A missing pathway is risk-stratifying admissions based on pre-screening event frequency: participants reporting 4+ episodes per month could be admitted for 6 weeks with high confidence of capture, while 2–3 episodes-per-month participants would enter the 10-week protocol, reducing average stay length while maintaining capture yield.

name: Computational Event-Triage Algorithm Complexity and Human-in-the-Loop Governance
consequences: A sophisticated multi-modal algorithm (integrating EEG spindle dynamics, skeletal movement amplitude, respiratory patterns, and arousal timing) can achieve high sensitivity for parasomnia event pre-flagging, reducing manual review burden by 40–50% and accelerating secondary analysis, but requires substantial computational infrastructure, validation against dual-rater ground truth across 100+ events, and ongoing model retraining as new participant cohorts are enrolled. A simpler rule-based triage system (e.g., flagging nights with movement amplitude exceeding participant-specific baseline plus concurrent arousal) is faster to deploy and interpretable to clinicians, but may miss atypical event morphologies and captures only 60–70% of true events, limiting its utility for automation.
options: Develop a multi-modal machine-learning event-triage algorithm using XGBoost or logistic regression, trained on dual-scored ground truth from months 12–24, with sensitivity and specificity testing against held-out test participants enrolled in months 24–30, requiring a dedicated data scientist allocation and 8–12 months of model development and validation

Deploy an initial rule-based flagging system by month 12 (movement amplitude + arousal co-occurrence) to reduce technician annotation burden in real-time, then transition to a hybrid model from month 18 onward where the rule-based system pre-flags candidate events and a human reviewer (technician or research assistant) confirms or rejects in 30–60 seconds, deferring full algorithmic sophistication to phase-two work

Establish a human-annotation-centric protocol where all nights are reviewed by two independent raters using AASM criteria without algorithmic pre-filtering through month 24, generating high-quality ground truth; from month 24 onward, retrospectively train a triage algorithm on the accumulated dual-scored data and pilot it prospectively on subsequent cohorts, prioritizing ground-truth quality over early automation speed
review: The tension is automation speed and technician burden reduction versus model validity and interpretability. All three options assume that the algorithm will be applied prospectively to in-coming participant data, but none explicitly addresses the governance question of who decides when an algorithm's confidence score is insufficient to flag an event — if thresholds are set conservatively to maximize specificity, sensitivity drops and annotation burden remains high; if set aggressively, false-positive flags create work for human reviewers. A missing pathway is threshold multiplicity, where separate algorithms are tuned for different event types (sleepwalking vs. confusional arousals vs. sleep terrors) with type-specific sensitivity/specificity trade-offs reported separately, reflecting the heterogeneity of parasomnia morphology but adding complexity to the deployment framework.

name: Stay Duration Ceiling and Re-admission Cycle Strategy
consequences: Capping individual stays at 10 weeks directly affects bed utilization, cost per captured event, and recruitment throughput; longer stays increase yield per participant but reduce the number of individuals enrolled, while shorter cycles with re-admission options spreads recruitment burden across more calendar time. Extending stays beyond 8 weeks begins to incur diminishing returns because the same technician team must continue annotation regardless of event frequency, and the accommodation cost per productive event rises steeply for low-yield participants.
options: Enforce a strict 6-week maximum stay with zero re-admission, accepting that 20–25% of enrolled participants yield zero events, to maximize annual recruitment volume and reduce per-participant facility burden even if overall capture rate declines.

Maintain the planned 10-week ceiling but require a 2-week washout off-site before any participant is eligible for re-admission, spreading re-admissions across future recruitment windows and preventing a single 'difficult' participant from occupying a suite for 20 consecutive weeks.

Implement a data-driven re-admission trigger: participants with zero events after 4 weeks undergo a joint review with the PI, bed partner, and psychologist; if the assessment indicates suppressed behavior due to the environment rather than low event frequency, offer a 4-week re-admission in a different suite with modified pre-sleep routine; otherwise discharge.
review: The core tension is between maximizing event yield per dollar and maintaining facility throughput; longer stays compound the risk that a single low-yield participant blocks a bed for months while newer recruits could fill it. All three options assume event capture is the primary bottleneck, but miss the possibility that annotation backlog — not bed availability — becomes the true constraint if technician staffing or dual-rater scheduling cannot keep pace with event volume.

name: Matched Control Enrollment Proportion and Measurement Depth
consequences: Matched controls consume facility suites, staffing, and annotation resources without generating primary event-capture data; enrolling 1 control per 2–3 parasomnia participants (roughly 15–20 controls over the program) validates the event-triage algorithm's false-positive rate and establishes normal-behavior baselines, but halves available beds for symptomatic recruitment. Conversely, enrolling fewer controls (1 per 4–5 participants) reduces algorithm confidence but frees suites for higher-yield parasomnia admissions.
options: Enroll matched controls at a 1:2 ratio (approximately 20 controls total) with full-stay monitoring identical to parasomnia participants, providing robust sensor false-positive rates and arousal baselines but deferring 10–15 symptomatic admissions across the 3-year window.

Recruit a single 6-person control cohort in year one, obtain their complete longitudinal data, then use that fixed reference dataset for all algorithm benchmarking in years two and three, freeing all subsequent suites for parasomnia recruitment and avoiding the repeated opportunity cost of control admissions.

Conduct 3–4 evening laboratory visits with matched controls (no overnight stay): obtain one night of full PSG under standard conditions in the clinical sleep lab, plus brief actigraphy and questionnaire data, enabling cheaper normative comparison without residential facility burden, though sacrificing the naturalistic domestic-environment measurement depth.
review: The tension lies between algorithm generalizability (more controls improve specificity) and recruitment efficiency (controls don't yield parasomnia events). None of these options adequately addresses the possibility that 'matched' controls should be selected on objective criteria (sleep architecture, arousal frequency, body position during sleep) rather than just age and sex, which could reveal that some matched controls actually harbor undiagnosed parasomnia-like movements themselves.

name: Inter-Rater Reliability Audit Frequency and Escalation Pathway
consequences: Dual-rater scoring of every event ensures Aim 3 validity but creates a labor bottleneck and dependency on two expert scorers' calendar availability; spotchecking (e.g., 30% of events scored by both raters, remainder by single rater) reduces cost and scheduling friction but risks drift in annotation standards and missed systematic rater bias. The choice also shapes whether scoring happens real-time (during the participant's stay, allowing rapid event-escalation decisions) or post-stay (cheaper, more reflective, but decouples feedback from clinical decision-making).
options: Score 100% of flagged events by both independent raters in real time during the participant's stay, resolve disagreement via adjudication meeting within 48 hours, and immediately escalate any confirmed event to trigger a full-PSG escalation night; accept that scheduling two expert raters nightly across 9 suites will require hiring a third rater or accepting 1–2 week scoring delays that push clinical escalation decisions into future stays.

Implement a tiered audit: technician flags potential events in real time and assigns a preliminary confidence score; only events scoring above 70% confidence on the technician's scale are immediately dual-scored during the stay; all remaining flags are batch-scored post-stay by both raters, with inter-rater disagreement resolved asynchronously, sacrificing real-time escalation capability but dramatically reducing concurrent scoring load.

Conduct monthly inter-rater reliability audits (blind recount of 20–30 previously scored events) to detect drift, but score all current events with a single designated rater in real time and rotate the 'primary rater' role every 6 months to prevent individual bias from dominating; resolve major discrepancies (>10% disagreement on audit) by bringing in the second rater for re-training or protocol clarification.
review: The core tension is between scientific rigor (dual scoring prevents individual rater bias) and operational speed (single-rater annotation is faster and cheaper). All three options assume that event-flagging accuracy is the bottleneck, but overlook the likelihood that the technician's real-time confidence score itself depends on training quality and fatigue — a night technician working a 10-hour shift may flag fewer subtle events than a fresh post-hoc rater, making real-time dual-scoring an unreliable proxy for true event frequency.

name: RBD Protocol Separation and Neurologic Screening Intensity
consequences: RBD requires dedicated neurologic screening (presence of motor signs, cognitive status, medication history, family history of neurodegeneration) distinct from NREM parasomnia workup; running RBD under a separate protocol with its own ethics approval and neurologic evaluation safeguards clinical integrity but fragments the facility's cohort, complicates staffing (night technicians must now know two distinct exclusion/escalation pathways), and risks low RBD enrollment if the neurologic gatekeeping is stringent. Integrating RBD into the main protocol streamlines operations but dilutes the NREM-focused phenotyping and may expose the unit to adverse events (e.g., a participant with prodromal Lewy body disease experiencing nocturnal falls) if neurologic risk stratification is not equally rigorous.
options: Maintain RBD as a strictly separate protocol with mandatory neurology co-screening by a movement disorder specialist before enrollment, restricting RBD participants to those with confirmed dream-enactment episodes and excluding any with red flags for neurodegenerative disease (cognitive complaints, gait abnormality, tremor, orthostatic hypotension); accept that RBD enrollment will likely be 5–10 participants if neurologist availability is limited.

Integrate RBD into the main protocol but mandate a brief standardized neurology assessment (Montreal Cognitive Assessment, gait observation, medication review) for all RBD candidates as part of the pre-admission interview; if red flags emerge, refer back to the recruiting neurologist for clearance before residential admission, allowing faster enrollment flow while maintaining safety checks without a second full ethics approval.

Defer RBD enrollment entirely until year two after the NREM unit has operated safely for 12 months; use year two to retrospectively review captured NREM events for any incidental REM-sleep motor signs, and then propose a targeted RBD protocol expansion with clearer RBD-specific phenotyping criteria and neurologist partnerships already established.
review: The core tension is between clinical safety (RBD patients can harbor occult neurodegenerative disease) and operational complexity (a separate protocol requires duplicate ethics, recruitment, and technician training). All options assume that the neurologic screening burden is acceptable and feasible, but none directly address the resource cost of embedding a movement disorder specialist in the pre-admission pathway or the likelihood that most RBD candidates will have already seen a neurologist — the real question is whether the unit should invest in bringing those external neurologists into its orbit or simply accept referrals from doctors who have already cleared the patient.

name: Enhanced-Night PSG Scheduling and Predictive Event Timing
consequences: Full PSG montage on the first, mid-stay, and final nights plus event-triggered escalation nights provides clinical-grade recording but creates logistical planning: PSG setup takes ~45 minutes, requires a trained technician, ties up a full electrode set, and generates large data files that must be processed and archived. If most participants' events cluster on nights 1–3 (adaptation effect), the scheduled 'mid-stay' PSG becomes wasteful; if events are uniformly distributed, the fixed schedule misses 90% of events and relies entirely on escalation-night captures. Conversely, trying to predict event timing based on pre-stay questionnaires or early-stay actigraphy patterns adds analysis overhead but could concentrate PSG resources on high-probability nights.
options: Adhere to the fixed schedule (first, mid-stay, final night) regardless of observed event clustering, accepting that some enhanced-night PSGs capture no events, to maintain a consistent data-collection structure that simplifies technician planning and enables between-participant EEG comparison at standardized timepoints in the stay.

Track actigraphy and mattress-sensor motor activity patterns during the first 7 days; if a participant shows clustering of large-movement events (>3 events per night) on specific days of the week or times of night, reallocate the mid-stay PSG to a predicted high-activity night and reserve the final-night PSG as a wash; if no pattern emerges, use the standard schedule, adapting PSG placement to observed participant rhythm rather than a fixed calendar.

Reduce the standard PSG schedule to first and final night only, then deploy full PSG only after a technician-flagged event on any night; this concentrates equipment use on confirmed event nights, reduces setup overhead and data-processing burden, but risks missing context events that occur on adjacent nights and may bias the dataset toward easily flagged events while missing subtle arousals.
review: The core tension is between standardized measurement (fixed PSG schedule ensures all participants have comparable physiological data) and efficient resource allocation (event-triggered PSG concentrates expensive monitoring on high-yield nights). All three options treat PSG nights as if they are equally valuable for Aim 2 (phenotyping), but overlook the fact that year-one participants serve as a de facto pilot cohort whose stay patterns will inform year-two scheduling — the strategy should perhaps include a formal mid-program review of event timing before finalizing year-two PSG protocols.

name: Technician Training Depth and Event-Flagging Authority Model
consequences: Night technicians are responsible for real-time event flagging, which directly shapes which events enter the annotation pipeline and which are missed; deeper training (20–40 hours of parasomnia-recognition instruction, supervised practice flagging, monthly continuing education) increases sensitivity but extends hiring-to-productivity timelines and creates dependence on a small number of highly trained staff; delegating flagging authority broadly (any technician can flag) speeds onboarding but risks low specificity and annotation bottleneck as false positives flood the review queue. The choice also determines whether technicians are empowered to make clinical escalation decisions (e.g., calling a supervisor if a participant is injured or distressed) or only to flag events and log observations for review.
options: Hire sleep medicine technicians (registry-certified Polysomnographic Technologists) with existing sleep-lab experience, requiring that new hires complete a 30-hour parasomnia-focused bootcamp before independent night shifts, and establish that technician event flags have high presumptive authority — if a tech flags an event, it automatically triggers dual-scoring and is considered valid until proven otherwise by raters.

Recruit from general nursing or healthcare backgrounds and implement a structured 60-day onboarding including 10 hours of video-module instruction (parasomnia recognition, patient safety, equipment troubleshooting), paired mentoring shifts with the senior tech, and a graded authority model: junior techs can flag obvious events but escalate ambiguous observations to the senior tech on duty or the on-call sleep physician, reducing false-positive load and preventing inexperienced staff from over-calling subtle movements.

Establish a hybrid model: hire one registry-certified polysomnographer (40 hours per week) as the senior night lead responsible for quality control, event adjudication, and escalation decisions; pair this person with two part-time technicians (25 hours per week each) who handle monitoring, logging, and patient care but must confirm any flagged event with the senior tech before it enters the scoring queue, concentrating expertise at a single point while maintaining 24/7 coverage.
review: The core tension is between hiring speed (general healthcare backgrounds onboard faster) and event detection accuracy (experienced sleep techs recognize subtle parasomnias). All three options underestimate the overhead of continuous training: parasomnias are rare events, so even a well-trained technician may flag fewer than 5 events per month, making it hard to maintain proficiency; the real challenge is whether the unit can afford to pay technicians enough to retain experienced staff and keep their skills sharp through regular feedback loops.

name: Data Repository Choice and Third-Party Validation Access
consequences: Depositing de-identified physiological data in a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, PhysioNet) accelerates community validation of the event-triage algorithm and attracts external researchers, but requires stripping all metadata that might indirectly identify participants (season of admission, suite number, rare comorbidities) and creates a one-way loss of control over how data are reused. Conversely, hosting data on a university-controlled server with formal data use agreements preserves institutional oversight and revenue potential (licensing fees, collaborative arrangements) but limits external pressure to maintain data quality and slows algorithm validation because external researchers must negotiate access individually.
options: Deposit the de-identified dataset in OpenNeuro under a CC-BY-4.0 license within 12 months of study completion, making all physiological waveforms and event annotations fully public; accept that external researchers will independently validate and potentially improve the event-triage algorithm, which may accelerate community adoption but also dilutes the team's publication advantage and removes any gating mechanism for future algorithm iterations.

Restrict data to researchers with a signed data use agreement through the University of Bonn's data-sharing portal, requiring external users to cite the original Bonn publications and to share their validation results with the core team before publication; maintain institutional control and foster collaborative improvement, but accept slower external uptake and the administrative burden of reviewing and approving each external access request.

Adopt a hybrid approach: release a 'methods validation' dataset (matched controls plus 10 representative parasomnia participants with anonymized demographics) to OpenNeuro immediately after first publication, enabling algorithm validation on the core phenotypes; reserve the full longitudinal dataset (all 50–70 participants) for internal use and data use agreement partnerships only, preserving granular data for future multi-site comparison while sharing enough to support reproducibility.
review: The core tension is between rapid external validation (public data accelerate algorithm scrutiny) and institutional data stewardship (restricted access maintains control over interpretation). None of these options directly addresses the practical friction that external researchers will need not just the waveforms but detailed metadata about sleep staging, artifact patterns, and technical settings — minimal metadata privacy may paradoxically slow validation rather than accelerate it if outsiders cannot reproduce the technician's real-time flagging context.
